title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body:
U.S. Air Force Academy ☑
U.S. Naval Academy ☑
I think I might know the answer... but where should I tour next? 👀
author: @SamEckholm

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: 60,000 start an application; 20,000 complete an application; there are 5,350 Congressional nominations and an unspecified number of Vice-Presidential nominations; 1,400 receive a LOA; 1,250 accept their LOA of whom 200-250 are recruited athletes and 50-75 are prior Enlisted and USNA Prep School.  Congrats to USNA Class of 2029 LOA recipients.  You earned it.  Go Navy!
author: @JohnTheMac

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: I was a hockey player at Navy, and when a new pair of gloves costs a month’s pay, it could get pricey. Carlos in the cobbler shop would repair my gloves for me whenever I wore a hole in the palms, glad to see him get a shoutout!
author: @kev_in17

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: My Dad was a naval submariner in the US Navy for over 26 years. He went to the Naval Academy and graduated with the class of 1954! He graduated with honors and was a Battalion Commander, a five striper. When we lived on the east coast, we often visited the academy and even went to Philadelphia for the Army/Navy football game a couple of times, where I met Admiral Hyman Rickover, the father of the nuclear navy. Dad passed 15 years ago and is interred in the columbarium at the Naval Academy. It is a fitting resting place for one of my heroes, my Dad! Love you and miss you, Dad!
author: @reggierico

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: as someone born and raised in the Annapolis area i absolutely love what navy has brought and the strength i feel in that city
author: @jacksoncross3863

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: My dad is class of ‘91. He then went back to teach mathematics at the Academy. I was born while he was teaching and lived in Annapolis for the first couple years of my life
author: @danfsteeple

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: Mind blown and hats off on the only one cobbler in the academy😳! I hope my son and the entire brigade are grateful to this wonderful man!
author: @mjgolfgirl0408

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: You probably don’t remember but I got a picture with you at the Memphis game! Crazy to think a little over a year ago my mom and I were watching your videos. Your videos were what introduced me to service academies in the first place. I was fortunate enough to get into USMA, USAFA, and USNA, and I chose USNA cuz we’re obviously better! Go Navy, beat Air Force!
author: @Sacchith

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: There will NEVER be any sports rivalry bigger than Army/Navy.  Growing up as the son of a 34-year Navy veteran, that Saturday in December was like a religious holiday in my house.  I still carry on that tradition today in my own home.  Love you and miss you, Dad.  Go Navy!!!! Beat Army!!!!
author: @douglasiles2024

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: I had the opportunity to work with many midshipmen, aviators, seals, and officers. I am very impressed. They are focused, methodical, and disciplined. Their personality are calm, and responsible. They also get the work done right and give feedback. They follow procedures and checklists thoroughly.
author: @william2chao

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: My father is interred in a niche at the Columbarium; the USNA was one of the most important and memorable times of his life and where he wanted his final resting place to be. Class of 1943 RIP pops, miss you every day.❤
author: @Crymeariver227

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: I graduated from the Naval Postgraduate School in Monterey , California. We did a visit to Annapolis Naval Academy back in 1993. Great memories !  Be all you can be !!!

I had to do a double take when I saw you filming everyone walking into King Hall! Love the video and I hope to be there for real soon (knock on wood)!

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: I am a graduate of the A.S. Popov Naval Institute of Radioelectronics in Petergof, Russia. Heaven and earth — that’s the difference. But still, I am glad I studied there. I wish sailors peaceful skies above, a fair wind, and seven feet under the keel! Peace to the world!

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: Being an Annapolitan native the Naval Academy is what I grew up with. It started on 1973 with sponsoring 13 mids and adding more over the years.me well I served 6 years in the Army.

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: 20 years ago, I was recruited to row at the naval academy. At the last moment I decided on "traditional college". I wish to this day I had decided to go. If you get the chance, do it!

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: I live in Annapolis and I'll see midshipman out and about with their computers.  They're really serious about their studies and have very busy schedules.  That said, I sincerely hope they enjoy their time here.  They are doing the country proud that's for sure.  Both of my parents worked at the Naval Academy.  I was able to visit Bancroft Hall several times (not the dorms).

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: I am from Uruguay, 85. I did visit the US Naval Academy at Annapolis MD back in 1963, while touring the States. I saw imposing old, grey buildings which obviously are no longer there, and some graceful sailboats moored near the premises. An ideal place to study everything related to ships. Thks for the fine video.

author: @digaoap

title: Inside the U.S. Naval Academy | What Makes Annapolis So Elite?
body: I’m from the Annapolis area. I wouldn’t say Downtown Annapolis feels like a typical college town. It feels firstly like a historic town where you’re stepping back in time. Very family oriented as well as artsy. Then there’s the unique atmosphere the Naval Academy brings—it’s a college yet it’s a military base.⚓️🦀
author: @carlygrace2

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: VMI Alum here. Believe it or not, the popularity of military academies go through cycles. Back in the 70s, these type of institutions were "disliked" among the young college youth. In 2023, it is also mocked and questioned. However, with enough time, this leadership pipeline will be great again as the results are always consistent. For the most part, whenever I meet a fellow graduate from the Naval Academy, Norwich, Texas A&M, or even the Merchant Marine Academy, I know I can trust them to be a hardworking leader with a moral compass. Our similar experience is what bonds us I think.
author: @amosusername

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: I think there is a lot of value in the training, especially for promising student straight out of highschool.  It kills a lot of bad habits and lazyness that a regular college will let you get away with. Keeps you fit and helps build mental toughness. But the service itself? I have no comment
author: @askjeeves28

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: My dear nephew is featured at 1:24:00. He is an exceptional young man - intelligent, kind, hard working, disciplined ... I couldn't be more proud. These brave young women and men deserve all the respect and accolades bestowed upon them. Go ARMY!!!
author: @JesusIsKingJohn316

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: When I was in the Army the guys from West Point always had their act together. Our company sent a platoon of men down to the platoon confidence course run by the special forces in Bad Tolz, Germany. There were four other platoons at the camp, one of them was the 509th Airborne. There was a scored competition between the platoons in the course as we did activities like escape and evation, land navigation, rock climbing, and small unit tactics. We beat the 509th Airborne in that competition, and it was largely due to the leadership of the butter bar lieutenant from West Point.
author: @GeorgePalmer-m8m

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: Looking to join the military when I graduate high school so this documentary was helpful thanks for an awesome documentary

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: i think high school students unsure of what direction to go in should try one of the military bootcamps. my older brother finally did it after a year on and off with community college and that really helped him find his path after becoming a marine.
author: @Koraeffect

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: You left out the Va Tech Corps of Cadets. i graduated in 1973 and we had a transfer cadet from WP (who had to to through our rat system) & he said VTCC was tougher then WP.  Nevertheless, Im grateful for the experience I  had as I had shown up as a lazy HS grad. The VTCC pounded discipline and good study habits into me that carried me through a successful life & career after my Army service as a Financial Advisor. Even if you aren't career military oriented, the VTCC has a great Civilian Track Option.  I highly recommend any military college (WP, Annapolis, etc)  or military/civilian college (like VTCC or TXAM or Norwich). Go Army!
author: @user-zf7jn7yr7r

title: Inside 6 Of The Most Intense Military Colleges In America, From West Point To Annapolis | Boot Camp
body: 44:53 What VMI calls "straining", we call "bracing" at The Citadel. It's an exaggerated position of attention. When done correctly, it improves your posture over time. Most of these cadets were committing a common rookie mistake. You do not lean back. That will only put undue stress on your back. The female cadet was the only one doing it correctly.

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things You Must Do On Campus
body: Professor Emeritus, here. You think we don't do research--but let me assure you that USNA has generously supported faculty research for decades. You see the result via the classroom because USNA teachers as a rule make it work that way: the Mids come first. What we learn, we share. Often mids collaborate with us in our research. There's a reason USNA is ranked with the very best four-year colleges.
author: @robertmadison2752

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things You Must Do On Campus
body: Time flies.  Some of these pictures are of my class.  The Academy is only a stepping stone to a career that will involve so many different experiences when you hit the fleet.  So many things happen after you hit the fleet that make it seem so long ago, yet, at the same time many vivid memories of it make it seem like it was yesterday.
author: @mmx2731

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things You Must Do On Campus
body: One thing I can tell every one who has been appointed to USNA -- no matter how ready you believe you are for what lies ahead -- YOU AREN'T!! Don't let that discourage you. Plebe Summer  will challenge you in ways you cannot currently imagine. The Brigade's return will will challenge you in different ways -- and for much longer. When Plebe Year is over -- then you'll (probably) be ready.

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things You Must Do On Campus
body: The Company Officer worked 8 to 5, daily, and seldom interfaced with you - that was the job of the 1st Classmen.  The Company Officer handled administrative responsibilities within the company and battalion.  My Company Officer left us alone.  Had a classmate for four years who thought his shit didn't stink and let us all know it.  He graduated low in our class but not the anchorman and somehow got to flight school and was killed two years later while showboating.  None of us went to his funeral - on purpose.  legacy classmates were dangerous because their relatives who attended Annapolis tipped them off to everything they needed to know to get by and keep the upperclassmen off their backs.  These jokers eventually made bad officers because you can't teach deportment, you learn it.
author: @justafanintexas7913

body: 1:20 is that what am going to find when I go to naval academy? oh lord bless  me and give me strength hahah

author: @werdnaizzy

title: US Naval Academy Annapolis
body:
@kotbegemot11 
Yea it def sucked. Just the way the military rolls. Either they never have enough or they have way to many. Either way, it's either a huge influx or outflux of personnel. It was all due to higher raised scores through API or "Aviation Pre-Indoctrination" Normal scores would be 80+ over 6 tests. For us it was raised to a 94+ or NSS score of 43+. Hardest shit I'v ever done in my life and I think anyone who went through it would agree.
author: @The-Rod-of-Light

title: Decoding the Navy Cryptologic and Cyber Communities: Your Guide to the Information Warfare Community
body: I am a BDCP applicant for the CWO designator who is currently majoring in Cybersecurity. Thank you so much for this informational video, the BDCP program does not have the option for MCWO so I thought to myself CWO would be the best chance to do cyber/ it would be related. Thanks to you I have a far better insight that even though I won't necessarily be doing much in relation to my degree I am still so excited at the possibility of becoming a CWO due to your explanation on the job/ lifestyle, how deployable, and the different types of billets like direct support, being assigned to a ship etc. This has been vital for me so thank you again Grant.

title: Decoding the Navy Cryptologic and Cyber Communities: Your Guide to the Information Warfare Community
body: Retired Cryptologic Warfare Officer (O5) here who lived the evolution from Cryptology to IWO to CWO. I was one of those who enjoyed the SIGINT work but did cyber just for the career broadening. I hated cyber, but it was the flavor of the day. Splitting it off was the right move and should have been done years earlier.

title: What New Navy Plebes Go Through On Their First Day At Annapolis | Boot Camp | Business Insider
body: It's funny watching this after watching the one on Paris Island. For the marines everyone looks all sad, depressed everyone's yelling, but the navy's over here smiling, laughing and shaking hands.
author: @TristanBottorff

title: What New Navy Plebes Go Through On Their First Day At Annapolis | Boot Camp | Business Insider
body:
As someone mentioned, you may need to go to Naval Academy Prep before heading off the the actual academy. It's a good chance to polish your skills before you arrive for plebe summer and your plebe year (freshman). The academy is a very challenging place and not everyone makes it thru.
Joining the Navy was one of my best decisions. Sometimes I was happy as heck to be there, other times I wanted out. There are a lot of ups and downs but the key is to stay with it and listen to what you are told.
I did go to college and I did get an education. But the Navy did a better job at preparing me for real life.
author: @13bcoffee

title: What’s going on at the US Naval Academy? Via: navybarstool on IG #navy #marine #military #sailor
body: Here's what happened: they have a ceremony right before graduation called "grog bowl" where they name each other's infractions committed throughout the year, if the person in charge deems it valid, the person that committed the infraction must drink this nasty slushy composed of random food that everyone brought. When it became these 2 girls turn, the girl standing still named a infraction, and it got very personal and hit a very sensitive nerve, and the brunette girl gets very emotional and refuses to drink the slushy and start challenging the other girl to fight, she walks over to the open area, motioning for the other girl to follow, wanting to physically beat her up, but the other girl had no desire to fight, and the brunette girl walks back to her and gets in her face and tries to intimidate her and fight, the other girl tries to calm her down and explains it wasn't personal, and at this time the nasty slushy gets poured on her because she is breaking the rules and refusing to drink the slushy and getting emotional and trying to act tough.
author: @alexmarlow2508

title: What’s going on at the US Naval Academy? Via: navybarstool on IG #navy #marine #military #sailor
body: @bryanthegoalie5692 I think has it figured out. For those unaware, the Navy and Marine Corps have a custom called “mess night” which is an informal ceremony dedicated to… buffoonery. There’s two punch bowls full of “grog”, one being an alcoholic mix and the other being an evil, malicious blend of literally any food the organizers can get their hands on (imagine sardines blended with burgers and tacos etc.) Participants call out others for “infractions” of goofy shit they’ve done or mistakes they’ve made in the past year or so (or for violating specific rules of the night, such as eating before the guest of honor). If the president of the mess decides it’s a valid charge, the defendant has to drink from the grog. It looks like something got personal here, the girl on the right took it too seriously and refused to drink the grog/lashed out on the chick to the left, then just got the grog dumped on her!
author: @ifhousesweremonkeys

title: What’s going on at the US Naval Academy? Via: navybarstool on IG #navy #marine #military #sailor
body: Fairly certain there is a grog bowl... And during these festivities someone made a foul. Instead of doing the punishment she went and chewed someone out for nomination. The nomination was in and she didn't proceed to get her grog choosing to argue with the (cadet?)female. So... A member of the ceremony brought it to her.
author: @bryanthegoalie5692

title: What’s going on at the US Naval Academy? Via: navybarstool on IG #navy #marine #military #sailor
body:
Looks like the ceremony called a "dining IN", as opposed to a more formal dining out. In either scenario, there's a feature called "the grog bowl" made from about anything you fathom a person can semi-safely drink.

The custom is to call someone forward to drink from the grog bowl. The person called upon is to drink from it, one cup, and when the cup is empty it is held upside down over the head. In some cases, if the person who called someone to the grog bowl is in the wrong - and the person called can give some evidence or get others to testify to it on the spot, this happens.
author: @sentinel_RevFoXX

title: What Navy Recruits Go Through In Boot Camp | Boot Camp | Business Insider
body: When I was in the navy boot camp, we got punished a lot.  Like, did not stand correctly, Group punishment, everyone start doing push ups until I tell you to stop.  But I remember this moment very well.  One instructor came in our squadron, told us if you are twins, they need to step out.  She verbally abused and threatened us with group punishment because no twin stepped out. She finally opened up the binder she came in with, called out 2 names cuz they have the same last name and same birthday.  And we saw one black guy and one white guy step out.    It was the funniest shit I have ever seen
author: @ryusukekapokurachi

title: What Navy Recruits Go Through In Boot Camp | Boot Camp | Business Insider

title: What Navy Recruits Go Through In Boot Camp | Boot Camp | Business Insider
body: The hardest thing when you separate out of the Navy, is getting back to that individual mindset. You're taught teamwork your whole time in. When you do get out. You'll be motivated, dedicated, and do as you were taught: to work as a team, and to take ownership. You'll go out of your way to help coworkers, family, etc, and you'll volunteer to stay late at work, and take accountability when things go wrong. You'll then realize that people won't ever do the same for you, they don't care that you do these things, and that there is a huge lack of responsibility and integrity in the civilian sector. You'll get screwed over a few times until the day you finally accept that you need to go back to thinking of yourself first and foremost prior to worrying about others.
author: @ru8enV

title: What Navy Recruits Go Through In Boot Camp | Boot Camp | Business Insider
body: For anyone who has a recruit just barely going in to the boot camp, make sure to send them their letters. title: What Day One As A Marine, Army Soldier, And Border Patrol Officer Is Really Like | BI Marathon
body: I graduated from boot camp at Ft. Sill, Ok a little over 20 years ago. They put us 100 deep in a cattle car with no seats and no air conditioning. When we got off some of toughest dudes I had ever seen were immediately in our faces trying to wear us down. As a young 18 year old I had no idea what I had signed up for until that moment. My time in the Army was some of the best in my life and it made me into who I am today. Wouldn’t trade it for nothing

title: What Day One As A Marine, Army Soldier, And Border Patrol Officer Is Really Like | BI Marathon
body:
As a Veteran, I learned a number of things about serving this great Nation.
You conform to the military. The military does not conform to you, the individual.
Boot camp strips you down to the basics. It then builds you back to be part of the military.
author: @TelemarketerTroll

title: What Day One As A Marine, Army Soldier, And Border Patrol Officer Is Really Like | BI Marathon
body: Advice I got from someone who served was to get as much sleep as possible whilst on the bus, screw getting to know the person sitting next to you try and get whatever sleep you can, cause once you're off that bus you won't be getting sleep or much sleep at all during the time you arrive(BTW THIS IS MOSTLY FOR THOSE WHO WILL ARRIVE AT NIGHT TIME). He told me when he arrived there at the base camp location at close to 9:30pm it took them to 3:00 am to get into bed and guess what happened? they were woken up at 0500 2 hours after falling asleep and he straight up said it was hell.
author: @Titan.Fox56

title: What Day One As A Marine, Army Soldier, And Border Patrol Officer Is Really Like | BI Marathon
body: I know this is completely off subject, but does no one else see the absolutely gorgeous, candy striped lawns at some of these military institutions? Those lines! From one professional to another, brother landscaper, you have great skill and my utmost respect. Your skills on that mower rivals that of an artist with a passion and a paint brush! Respect.
author: @chrisnash1290

title: What Day One As A Marine, Army Soldier, And Border Patrol Officer Is Really Like | BI Marathon
body: To everyone in the comments talking about how “unnecessary” and “uncivilized” recruit training is: That’s the entire point. The instructors don’t just yell and scream for the hell of it, it’s by design. They need these recruits (especially those in the Marine Corps and Army) to be able to function in highly stressful and chaotic environments such as a combat zone. If they don’t learn how to operate in such environments, they will shut down and not be able to function and complete their mission when and if that time comes. Combat is ugly, and your leaders are not going to hold your hand and politely ask you to load your machine gun or grab the extra ammo when grenades are exploding and people are actively shooting at you.
author: @connorr.1763

title: What Day One As A Marine, Army Soldier, And Border Patrol Officer Is Really Like | BI Marathon
body: RTC Great Lakes, 1976 Co 941. There was no screaming and yelling the entire 13 weeks, we all did just fine, the Navy was great back then.

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things to Avoid
body: I tested out of all the physics requirements.  That gave me a head start and I got into a program that had me earning an MS in Aerospace Engineering (through U of MD) six months after graduating.  I then reported to flight school.  Most everybody I knew bought an Ensignmobile.  I did too, although I bought a relatively inexpensive older convertible which I still own and cherish some 37 years later.  My Grandson has dibs.
author: @rickprice6312

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things to Avoid
body: oh boy I wanna go to the Naval Academy that's my goal to get good grades in high school so I can do my dream
author: @n.i.t.f919

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things to Avoid
body: All very wise suggestions, esp. #1.  You are not special at USNA.  I don't recall being able to test out of core classes during my day, not sure how I feel about that.  Calculus class at USNA is very different than Calculus class at most other schools, for example.  I got a B+ in Calc I at the University of Southern California without studying much.  Very different story with Calc I at USNA.  I'd suggest not rolling that dice.  I did test out of foreign language requirements because I was an enlisted CTI (Russian) before I got into Annapolis.
author: @chrisakins692

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things to Avoid
body: I tested out of several classes to the point where I started on my major, immediately, and got targeted by the upperclassmen in my classes.  They even reached into my company to see to it that I caught hell. Invest the starter cash because you get transferred as fast as you get to your first duty station; exception is if you end up in flight school at Pensacola, where you are there for a while. I participated in two pranks that, to this day, have never been solved - nothing bad, just creative.  They are still talked about and the guys who are taking credit for them are real pieces of work.
author: @justafanintexas7913

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things to Avoid
body: Legacy people are protected. I tested out of 5 classes and it helped, except I ended up in class with some upperclassmen who resented it.  Be wary of the guys/gals coming in with you from NAPS, they have a head start on you and won't help you because that would not make them superior.
author: @justafanintexas7913

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things I Wish I Knew About Before Attending
body:
No matter how ready you "think" you are for Plebe Summer and what follows --- YOU AREN'T!!

Not only that, chances are that you and most of your new classmates will arrive as members of the top 3-4% of the academic achievers in the nationwide entering college Freshman class. You and your classmates will be hard against the far right end of the nationwide probability curve

Like it or not, you will now find yourself belonging somewhere under a new probability curve, consisting only of your classmates.

It will not be easy, nor should it be -- but you can do it!!

Good luck from an old salt who's a member of the Class of 1957 (the last class to have a real Plebe Year)
author: @navy57

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things I Wish I Knew About Before Attending
body: I knew 2 guys who went, neither one finished, both did well later in life, my brother was a Marine, after basic he was offered a chance to attend USNA prep school, he declined, he did well in the Marines over 4 yrs and was glad to leave, today he is very successful, 4 kids, 6 figure income and long marriage,
author: @michaelsix9684

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things I Wish I Knew About Before Attending
body: Joseph Steffan wrote a great book on the Academy, he was weeks from graduating in 1987, he was expelled for being gay, book is Honor Bound, well done, and hard to find now -- I applied in 1974, got put on the alternate list,  glad I was not accepted, it was all engineering then
author: @michaelsix9684

title: U.S. Naval Academy - 5 Things I Wish I Knew About Before Attending
body: Truer words were never stated.  The last part was key.  34 years later and I still consider my plebe year classmates as close friends.
